mcqs.question: Why is a Host OS compromise in a cloud environment more severe than a Guest OS compromise?

options.text: The Host OS is harder to patch
The Host OS has access to all VMs on the physical host
The Host OS runs more applications
The Host OS uses a different OS version

mcqs.explanation: The Host OS has the highest privilege level after the hypervisor and can access all VMs, virtual disks, and memory on that physical server. A Guest OS compromise is isolated to that single VM.

mcqs.question: Which access control model is implemented by SELinux and AppArmor?
mcqs.explanation: SELinux and AppArmor implement Mandatory Access Control (MAC) — system-enforced policies that override traditional DAC permissions. MAC is preferred for high-security cloud host environments.

mcqs.question: In the IaaS shared responsibility model, who is responsible for Guest OS patching?
mcqs.explanation: In IaaS, the provider secures the infrastructure (hypervisor, Host OS, physical hardware). The customer is responsible for the Guest OS, applications, data, and patching within their VMs.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of Linux namespaces in cloud security?
mcqs.explanation: Linux namespaces isolate process groups by PID, network, mount, and user namespaces. They are the foundation of container isolation (Docker, Kubernetes) — each container has its own namespace view of the system.

mcqs.question: Which side-channel attacks exploit shared CPU behavior to leak data across VM boundaries?
mcqs.explanation: Spectre and Meltdown are side-channel attacks that exploit speculative execution in CPUs to read data from other processes or VMs. They are OS-level vulnerabilities that affect multi-tenant cloud environments.
